title: Condemning woke foreign aid programs.
plain_summary: This bill criticizes foreign aid programs that the House thinks waste money on things like transgender health services and diversity programs in other countries. The House wants to put limits on how much money can be spent and make Congress approve bigger grants before they happen. They also want more watching over these programs and require the government to be more open about how the money is spent. The bill says these foreign aid programs are not good uses of taxpayer money.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Resolution Condemning Woke Foreign Aid Programs
sections.text: The House of Representatives lists many examples of foreign aid spending that it considers wasteful. These include two million dollars for transgender health services in Guatemala, twenty million dollars for an Iraqi version of Sesame Street called Ahlan Simsim, one and a half million dollars for workplace diversity programs in Serbia, and forty-five million dollars for diversity scholarships in Burma. Other examples include ten million dollars for male circumcision programs in Mozambique, two hundred fifty thousand dollars for gender equality workshops in Morocco, and one hundred thousand dollars for a drag show HIV awareness campaign in South Africa. Smaller amounts were spent on projects like eighty-five thousand dollars for a bee conservation musical in Brazil, seventy thousand dollars for a diversity musical event in Ireland, and various amounts for programs involving transgender, LGBTQ, and feminist themes in countries like Bolivia, Kenya, Chile, Peru, Vietnam, Colombia, Thailand, Pakistan, Ecuador, and India. The House declares it is firmly against funding these types of programs because it views them as wasteful spending of taxpayer money on unnecessary or politically driven projects. The House requests more oversight of federal aid programs and wants the Government Accountability Office to audit all grants given by the State Department and USAID since twenty twenty-one. It wants similar grants suspended until they can be reviewed to see if they match important national interests and responsible spending. The House demands that all grant applications and results be made public on a searchable website within ninety days of being awarded. It recommends redirecting money from these programs to domestic needs like fixing roads and bridges, veteran healthcare, or disaster relief. The House wants inspector generals to review grant effectiveness every year and report findings to Congress and the public. It proposes limiting cultural exchange and advocacy grants abroad to no more than one-tenth of one percent of the federal discretionary budget. The House wants all future grants over ten thousand dollars to need explicit approval from Congress through a streamlined review process. Finally, it plans to draft legislation that would ban using federal money for overseas programs that promote specialized social agendas unless they clearly and directly benefit United States national security or economic interests.
